role: Sr. SAFe Scrum Master / Team Coach
subtitle: SAFe · ADO · AI-Enhanced Delivery · Consultative Coaching
hero_sub: Ten years of coaching Agile teams in SAFe environments — consultative, flexible, AI-fluent, and action-oriented when the information is still incomplete.

the_case.need: 2+ years working in a SAFe environment
the_case.answer: Eptura (2022–2023): 2 years in a PI-aligned SAFe environment across ~10 distributed engineering teams. DigitasLBI/SunTrust (2014): SAFe enterprise, PI-aligned, SOX-compliant financial services.
the_case.detail: At Eptura I ran inside a SAFe cadence for two years — PI Planning cycles, ART coordination, Scrum-of-Scroms, and system demos across ~10 teams (India and California). At DigitasLBI I was embedded on-site at SunTrust in a SAFe enterprise program, coordinating PI-aligned dependencies across two teams and an offshore QA group. The SAFe delivery experience is documented and direct.

the_case.need: 1+ year hands-on ADO — facilitating Agile team practices in Azure DevOps
the_case.answer: Direct ADO experience at Crocs (2026), Eptura (2022–2023), and Understood.org (2019–2020). Board configuration, workflow management, sprint tracking, flow metrics, and delivery dashboards.
the_case.detail: ADO is a tool I configure and maintain, not just use. At Eptura I was the ADO configuration owner for the PMO — standardizing boards, workflows, and delivery health dashboards across ~10 teams. At Crocs I maintained ADO boards throughout a full release lifecycle and built real-time dashboards for leadership. At Understood.org I used ADO for delivery tracking in a regulated program with a globally distributed team.

the_case.need: Consultative approach — coaches to outcomes, leads with solutions, project-centric
the_case.answer: This is the operating mode, not an aspiration. Every engagement described: here is the problem, here is the solution, here is the plan. Not: let us form a working group and discuss Agile values.
the_case.detail: At Crocs I inherited a release mid-cycle and delivered it on time — no waiting for a perfect handoff, no process debate. At Understood.org I took over a distressed program in its final months and shipped clean. At Eptura I coached ~10 teams toward self-organization by giving them structure, not more meetings. Consultative and solutions-oriented is not a style I adopt — it is how delivery actually works.

the_case.need: Build and maintain ADO dashboards — flow metrics, KPIs, schedule and cost performance
the_case.answer: Eptura: built standardized ADO dashboards for ~10 teams (velocity, flow, predictability, team health). Crocs: real-time release readiness dashboards giving leadership visibility before they asked for it.
the_case.detail: I build the monitoring infrastructure — not just the reports. At Eptura I created the ADO and Smartsheet dashboards that became the PMO standard across all teams. At Crocs I built Release Radar, a real-time tracking framework that surfaced cross-environment dependencies and schedule risks before they threatened go-live. At Understood.org I administered ADO for a globally distributed program. Dashboards that tell a story leadership can act on, not just data exports.

the_case.need: AI tools applied within the SM role — backlog generation, documentation, analytics, reporting
the_case.answer: Jira AI embedded in backlog management and sprint summaries at Crocs. Microsoft Copilot in documentation and reporting. ~30% administrative overhead reduction with improved output quality. Active daily Claude and Copilot use.
the_case.detail: The requirement is 'familiarity with AI tools and demonstrated ability to apply them within the Scrum Master role.' At Crocs I did exactly this: embedded Jira AI into backlog grooming and sprint summary workflows, used Copilot for stakeholder documentation and reporting automation. The administrative overhead dropped ~30% and the output got sharper. I use these tools to compress the administrative side of SM work so coaching time goes to actual coaching.

the_case.need: Navigate ambiguity, create structure, be action-oriented without complete information
the_case.answer: Distressed program recovery at Understood.org. Greenfield platform build at OVRSTCH. AI startup delivery at Stealth-Mode AI. SAFe program coordination across ~10 teams at Eptura. All ambiguous. All shipped.
the_case.detail: Navigating ambiguity is a skill I have exercised repeatedly: taking over a distressed program in its final months (Understood.org), running delivery for an AI startup with shifting requirements and no prior process (Stealth-Mode AI), building a platform from scratch with concurrent unknown workstreams (OVRSTCH), and coordinating PI delivery across teams that had never operated in a scaled environment before (Eptura Marketing adoption). In each case: create structure, move toward the outcome, resolve information gaps in motion — not in a planning cycle.

the_stack.headline: The SAFe delivery ecosystem.
the_stack.subhead: The tools and cadences I operate in daily.

nodes.label: Azure DevOps (ADO)
nodes.desc: Primary SAFe delivery tool — board configuration, sprint tracking, flow metrics, delivery dashboards, KPI reporting
nodes.pm_role: ADO configuration owner at Eptura PMO (~10 teams). Real-time release dashboards at Crocs. Program-level delivery tracking at Understood.org. Not a user — a configurator.

nodes.label: SAFe / PI Planning
nodes.desc: Scaled Agile Framework — Program Increment planning, ART coordination, Scrum-of-Scroms, system demos, cross-team dependency management
nodes.pm_role: Ran inside SAFe PI cadences at Eptura (2 years, ~10 teams) and DigitasLBI (SAFe enterprise, SOX-compliant financial services). PI Planning, ART syncs, dependency mapping are established practice.

nodes.label: Jira & Jira AI
nodes.desc: Team-level delivery tracking — sprint boards, backlogs, velocity, burndown — plus AI-assisted backlog synthesis and reporting
nodes.pm_role: Built Jira from scratch at A&E (10+ teams). Used Jira AI at Crocs for backlog management and sprint summaries — compressed administrative work, improved output quality.

nodes.label: Microsoft Copilot
nodes.desc: AI-assisted documentation, stakeholder communication, and reporting
nodes.pm_role: Embedded at Crocs for SM documentation and reporting workflows. Reduces time on administrative output so coaching time goes to actual coaching.

nodes.label: Confluence & Smartsheet
nodes.pm_role: Standardized Jira/Confluence at Eptura. Built leadership-level Smartsheet reporting synthesizing delivery health across ~10 teams into a single dashboard view.

when_things_break.scenario: PI objectives are at risk mid-increment — multiple teams tracking yellow
response: Call an unscheduled ART sync within 24 hours — not at the next PI cadence
Triage: which objectives are recoverable, which need de-scoping, which need escalation?
Partner with Product Management on scope trade-offs — scope/time/quality — someone decides
Build a recovery plan with named owners and dates; publish to leadership immediately
Post-PI retrospective supplement: what planning assumptions were wrong?

when_things_break.scenario: Team is resisting the consultative coaching approach — wants prescriptive process
response: Separate the resistance from the request: are they asking for clarity, or avoiding ownership?
Give them a structured starting point — a working agreement, a sprint goal template, a DoD — then coach toward owning it
Name the outcome we're trying to reach and ask the team how to get there
Adjust the coaching style to where the team is, not where you want them to be
Escalate if the resistance is structural (leadership behavior undercutting the coaching)

when_things_break.scenario: ADO board is out of sync — sprint items not updated, backlog not groomed, dashboards stale
response: Fix it immediately — stale ADO is a signal problem before it is a delivery problem
Run a 30-minute board hygiene session with the team to reset current state
Identify the root cause: is the team updating verbally and not in ADO, or is work happening outside the board?
Add a board review to the standup format until hygiene is habitual
Build a lightweight ADO automation rule to flag items with no updates after 2 days

how_i_run_a_room: Ceremonies end with committed actions and named owners — not 'we'll follow up'
ADO boards are current before every standup — not updated after
Blockers surface in the standup and have owners within the hour — not at the next ceremony
PI Planning produces objectives the teams actually believe in, not ones that were imposed
AI tools compress my administrative time so coaching time goes to coaching
Stakeholder updates lead with the thing they need to act on — not background context
Conflict is addressed directly and quickly — it does not wait for a retrospective

before: ADO boards are stale — the data exists but nobody trusts it
PI objectives are aspirational — teams committed to them in the room but don't believe them
Blockers surface at retrospectives instead of standups — too late to protect the sprint
Coaching is about Agile values; outcomes are someone else's problem
after: ADO is the source of truth — boards are current, dashboards are accurate, leadership trusts the data
PI objectives are realistic and owned — teams built them and believe in them
Blockers surface in standups and have owners within the hour
Coaching is outcome-first — the team gets better at delivery, not just better at Agile

the_ramp.deliverable: ADO audit across assigned teams — current board health, workflow gaps, dashboard accuracy. Meet every team member, PO, and key stakeholder. Identify top 3 blockers and coaching priorities.
First full PI-aligned ceremony cycle facilitated. ADO improvement proposed. AI tools embedded in at least one SM workflow.
ADO dashboards reliable and publishing regularly. First retrospective with documented, tracked output. Direct coaching conversations underway where needed.
Teams running cleanly. ADO trusted by leadership. Coaching producing behavioral change. No handholding needed — clear escalation paths established.
